title: Workbook — Sin
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. What definition of St. Augustine on sin does the Catechism include? Why is the definition so broad (utterance, deed, desire)?
  2. Explain in your own words each part of the CCC 1849 definition: offense against reason, truth, right conscience, and failure in genuine love.
  3. What are the three conditions necessary for a sin to be mortal? Give an example of how each condition might be absent in a concrete case.
  4. What is the difference between mortal sin and venial sin? Use whatever metaphor you like (fracture vs. crack, shipwreck vs. seasickness, rupture vs. cooling...).
  5. List the seven capital sins and, next to each one, the virtue that combats it.
  6. What are «structures of sin»? Give a contemporary example.

For Reflection and Writing

1. St. Augustine said that in the theft of the pears he sought «a counterfeit of omnipotence.» What do you think he was really seeking? What do you seek when you do something you know is wrong?

Write honestly. Do not look for the «correct» answer. Look for the true one.

 

 

 

 

2. Read the parable of the prodigal son (Lk 15:11-32) in your Bible. Then write a short letter from the prodigal son to his father the day after the feast. What would he say? What feelings would he have?

 

 

 

 

 

 

3. Imagine someone says to you: «I don't believe in sin. What happens is that we make mistakes, like when a program has a bug.» How would you respond, using what you learned in this lesson?

 

 

 

 

 

4. Do a brief examination of conscience, reviewing in your life each of the seven capital sins. You do not need to write the details; simply put next to each one: «affects me,» «affects me a little,» or «does not affect me.»

Pride: ____ | Avarice: ____ | Envy: ____ | Wrath: ____ | Lust: ____ | Gluttony: ____ | Sloth: ____

Then choose ONE. The one that most affects you. Write a concrete action you will take this week to cultivate the contrary virtue. Be specific: «be more humble» does not count. «Apologize to X» or «listen without interrupting Y» does.

 

 

 

Practical Activities

A. The Line of Sin and Grace

Draw a horizontal line on a sheet of paper. At the far left write «Original Sin (Gen 3).» At the far right, «Heaven.» Now mark important points in salvation history: the Flood, the Tower of Babel, the Covenant with Abraham, the Exodus, King David (with his sin and repentance), the prophets... Arrive at the Cross of Christ — put it in the center, clearly visible. After the Cross, trace the line upward, toward Heaven. Write on one side, next to the Cross: «Where sin abounded, grace abounded all the more (Rom 5:20).»

B. The Seven Vices and Their Remedies

Make a two-column table:

Capital ViceRemedy (virtue + concrete action)
Pride
Avarice
Envy
Wrath
Lust
Gluttony
Sloth

C. Letter to My «Self» in Sin

Think of a situation in which you gave in to sin (it could be something recent or something past). Write a letter from your «present self» to your «self at that moment.» Do not write to scold yourself. Write to illuminate, with what you have learned in this lesson. What would you say to that person you were? What did you need to hear that you did not hear?

 

 

 

 

 

 

D. Preparing for Confession

Do a guided examination of conscience. Use the Ten Commandments as a guide (if you know them) or these questions: Have I rejected God in my life? Have I used his name disrespectfully? Have I missed Sunday Mass? Have I disobeyed my parents or just authorities? Have I harmed anyone physically or with words? Have I had impure thoughts or acts? Have I stolen or been unjust? Have I lied or slandered? Have I coveted what belongs to others? Have I desired another person in a disordered way?

Write (privately) the sins you want to confess. Then note the date and time of the next available Confession at your parish.

Memory Verse

«If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us. If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just, and will forgive our sins and cleanse us from all unrighteousness.»

— 1 John 1:8-9

Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus, my Savior,
look upon me, a sinner,
but do not let me fall into despair.
You who said to the adulterous woman: «Neither do I condemn you. Go, and sin no more,»
say the same to me.
Grant me the grace to acknowledge my sins with humility,
and the confidence to draw near to your mercy without fear.
I want to return to the Father's house.
Amen.

Print Workbook
